title.title: Recap
title.subtitle: Centralization, blockchain types, and the permissioned vs permissionless spectrum

centralization.heading: Centralized vs Decentralized vs Distributed
centralization.subtitle: Three network topologies — first described by Paul Baran (1964). Blockchain sits at the distributed end of the spectrum.

centralized.title: Centralized
points: Single authority controls everything — one hub, many spokes
Single point of failure — compromise the center and the whole system falls
Efficient and easy to govern, but requires full trust in the authority
Examples: Visa, central banks, AWS, Facebook

decentralized.title: Decentralized
points: Multiple control centers — each manages its own cluster of nodes
More resilient than centralized — but each sub-center is still a point of failure
Governance is federated — rules negotiated between centers
Examples: Internet DNS, Consortium blockchains, banking networks

distributed.title: Distributed
points: No center — every node is equal and connects directly to peers
No single point of failure — removing any node doesn't break the network
Trustless — consensus replaces authority. The rules are in the code.

permissioned.title: Permissioned Blockchains
permissioned.description: Private and consortium blockchains are designed for trust within known participant sets — not trustlessness at global scale.

what.title: 🔐 What makes them permissioned?
what.body: Participants are known and identity-verified before joining. Access control defines who can read, write, or validate. Consensus is faster (BFT variants) — no mining needed. Governance is agreed off-chain by the consortium.

private.title: 🏢 Private Blockchains
private.body: Controlled by a single organisation — internal use only. Highest performance, lowest decentralisation. Used for internal audit trails and cross-department data sharing. Example: Hyperledger Fabric in single-org mode.

consortium.title: 🤝 Consortium Blockchains
consortium.body: Governed by a group of organisations with shared interests. Each member runs nodes — shared validation. Used for trade finance, healthcare data, and supply chains. Examples: R3 Corda, TradeLens (Maersk + IBM).

tradeoff.title: ⚖️ The Trade-off vs Public Chains
tradeoff.lose: censorship resistance, open access, and trustlessness. Neither is better — they solve different problems.

keyPoints: Participants are known — identity replaces economic incentives as the trust mechanism
BFT consensus is orders of magnitude faster than PoW/PoS — no energy waste
Private chains maximise performance; consortium chains balance privacy with shared governance
Permissioned blockchains are not "less blockchain" — they are optimised for different constraints

items.option1: Open to anyone — no identity required
items.option2: Restricted — participants are vetted and known
BFT variants — pre-selected validators, much faster

items.option1: Full — anyone can audit every transaction
Trustless — math and economics enforce rules
items.option2: Trust in consortium governance and identity

items.option1: Public finance, censorship resistance, open dApps
items.option2: Enterprise supply chains, regulated finance, healthcare

usecases.heading: The Use of Blockchain
usecases.subtitle: When does blockchain actually add value — and when doesn't it?

use.title: ✅ Use blockchain when…
items: Multiple parties share data without trusting each other
An immutable audit trail is required
Intermediaries add cost without adding value
Rules need to be enforced automatically (smart contracts)
Censorship resistance or permissionless access is required

dontUse.title: ❌ Don't use blockchain when…
items: A central authority is already trusted by all parties
Data privacy is paramount — public chains expose everything
You control all participants — just use a database
On-chain data integrity can't be guaranteed (oracle problem)

takeaways.title: Section 00 — Key Takeaways
items: Centralization offers efficiency; decentralization offers resilience and trustlessness
Blockchain exists on a spectrum — from fully public/permissionless to fully private/permissioned
Blockchain adds value when multiple untrusting parties share data, or when intermediaries can be replaced by code
Permissioned blockchains trade censorship resistance for privacy, performance, and compliance
Consortium blockchains allow competing organizations to collaborate on shared infrastructure
There is no universally best blockchain — each is optimised for different constraints

ifNotYet.body: Find your teammates during the next break. Don't wait until Day 3 — you'll be playing catch-up.

todaysLink.label: Today's link
todaysLink.body: As you see Bitcoin / Ethereum / Hyperledger / etc — think which one would fit a pain point you've experienced.
